A serum lactic acid level BNP and heart failure • BNP (brain natriuretic peptide) is secreted from the ventricles in response to ventricular volume expansion and pressure overload. • Release is directly proportional to ventricular dysfunction and correlates with end-diastolic pressure. • BNP undergoes partial renal excretion; levels are inversely proportional to creatinine clearance. • Increases in both HFrEF and HFpEF, but typically to a (much) greater degree in HFrEF BNP and heart failure • BNP < 100 excludes CHF as a cause of dyspnea* • BNP > 400 confers a 95% likelihood of CHF • BNP 100 – 400 requires further investigation • Pulmonary disorders that can elevate BNP include lung cancer, cor pulmonale and pulmonary embolus • Other causes of BNP elevation include cirrhosis with ascites, primary hyperaldosteronism, Cushing disease • These disorders do not cause the same extent of BNP elevation as LV dysfunction *50 pg/mL suggested as upper limit of normal if BMI > 35; BNP levels are lower in obese patients, both with and without heart failure BNP and heart failure • For patients at risk of developing HF, natriuretic peptide biomarker–based screening followed by: • team-based care • including a cardiovascular specialist optimizing guideline- directed management can be useful to prevent the development of left ventricular dysfunction (systolic or diastolic) or new-onset HF. questions.source: Session 08 — AAFP Board Prep Poll Question
questions.stem: The specificity of a screening test is best described as the proportion of persons: Sensitivity and Specificity ▪ Both measures of a test’s validity – its ability to correctly detect people with or without the disease in question ▪ 4 logical possibilities in diagnostic testing Sensitivity and Specificity Disease Present Absent False Positive Positive True Positive Type 1 error (α error) False Negative Negative Type II error True Negative (β error) tluseR tseT a b c d Sensitivity ▪ Ability to detect people who do have the disease ▪ The % of people with a disease that is correctly detected or classified TP TP+FN X 100 Disease or + - + a b a/(a+c) - c d tluseR tseT Sensitivity ▪ Very sensitive tests are used for screening or ruling out disease ▪ If the result of a highly sensitive test is negative, it allows the disease to be ruled out with confidence ▪ SNOUT ▪ SeNsitive test with a Negative result rules OUT the disease Specificity ▪ Ability to detect people who do not have the disease ▪ The % of the disease-free people who are correctly classified or detected: TN FP+TN X 100 Disease or + - d/(b+d) + a b - c d tluseR tseT Specificity ▪ Very specific tests are therefore appropriate for confirming or ruling in the existence of a disease ▪ If the result of a highly specific test is positive, the disease is almost certainly present ▪ SPIN ▪ A Specific test with a Positive result rules IN the disease Sensitivity and Specificity Clinical Practice ▪ In clinical practice, sensitivity and specificity are inversely related: an increase in one causes a decrease in the other. ▪ This is because groups of patients with the disease and groups who are disease-free lie in a continuum, overlapping each other, rather than forming two distinctly different groups. ▪ The tester therefore has to select a cut-off point to make a diagnostic decision. Screening Tests Sequence ▪ Although there are tests of relatively high sensitivity and specificity for some diseases, it is often best to use a combination of tests when screening for/diagnosing a particular disease. Screening Tests Sequence ▪ A highly sensitive (and usually relatively inexpensive) test should be used first, almost guaranteeing the detection of all cases of the disease (albeit at the expense of including a number of false-positive results). ▪ This should be followed by a more specific test (and usually more expensive test) to eliminate the false-positive results. ▪ e.g. this is the usual sequence if testing for HIV, Hepatitis B, and many other common but serious diseases. Predictive Values ▪ Predictive values are essential to determine the extent to which screening tests can identify the likely presence or absence of a condition of interest so that their findings encourage appropriate decision-making. ▪ When the sensitivity of a test is known...  “Given that a patient has the disease, what is the ability of the test to discover this?

choices.A: with the condition who test positive
choices.B: with the condition who test negative
choices.C: without the condition who test positive
choices.D: without the condition who test negative

questions.source: Session 08 — AAFP Board Prep Poll Question
questions.stem: In a study to evaluate a test as a screen for the presence of a disease, 235 of the 250 people with the disease had a positive test and 600 of the 680 people without the disease had a negative test. Based on this data, the specificity of the test for the disease is: 2 x 2 Table Disease (+) Disease (-) Total Test (+) for disease 235 a b Test (-) for disease 600 c d Total 250 680 Disease (+) Disease (-) Total Test (+) for disease 235 80 315 Test (-) for disease 15 600 615 Total 250 680 930 The specificity of a test for a disease is the proportion or percentage of those without the disease who have a negative test. Disease (+) Disease (-) Total Test (+) for disease 235 80 315 a b Test (-) for disease 15 600 615 c d Total 250 680 930 ▪ Sensitivity – the proportion of those with the disease who have a positive test ▪ False negative rate ▪ Prevalence of the disease in the sample Calculation of PV ▪ PPV = TP/TP+FP x 100 = a/a+b X100 = 235/235+80(100) = 75% ▪ NPV = TN/FN+TN x 100 = d/c+d X100 = 600/15+600(100) = 97% Disease (+) Disease (-) Total Test (+) for disease 235 a 80 b 315 Test (-) for disease 15 c 600 d 615 Total 250 680 930 Sensitivity/Specificity PPV/NPV ▪ As the prevalence of the disease in a population increases • PPV increases • NPV decreases ▪ As the prevalence of the disease in a population decreases • PPV decreases • NPV increases Summary Measurement Equation 1. Relative Risk EER/CER = [a/(a+c)] / [b/(b+d)] 2. Absolute Risk CER-EER = [b/(b+d)] – [a(/a+c)] 3. NNT/NNS 1/ARR = 1/[b/(b+d)] – [a(/a+c)] 4 NNH 1/ARR = 1/[a(/a+c)] - [b/(b+d)] 5. Sensitivity a/(a+c) 6. Specificity d/(b+d) 7. Positive Predictive Value a/(a+b) 8. Negative Predictive Value d/(c+d) Thank You david.weismiller@unlv.edu Answers 1. A 2. A 3. B 4. B 5. D 6. C

questions.stem: A 27-year-old female with a BMI of 39 requests help with a diet. You tell her: 2-pound weekly weight loss results Diets • No diet has been shown to be better than others for weight loss • Commercial plans with best evidence = Weight Watchers, Optifast, Jenny Craig, Nutrisystems • Satiety plans (AmIHungry.com, Volumetrics, Wondr) successful long term • Raw food, vegetarian, paleo diets are all effective due to reduced calories • Fasting–safe in type 2 diabetes but not in type 1 (same for religious fasts)* • Portfolio diet: “plant forward” (whole grains, fruits, vegetables, nuts--discouraging animal proteins • Mediterranean diet and DASH (not weight loss diets) are healthiest* • Decrease risk of diabetes, cardiovascular disease, peripheral vascular disease • Improve diabetes even in the absence of weight loss • Very low-carbohydrate diets (keto) • Fastest and reduce insulin resistance; that edge is lost by 6 months • In patients with HTN, obesity, prediabetes, and DM resulted in greater improvements in systolic BP, glycemic control, and weight loss (Ann Fam Med 2023;21:256-263) Children and Teens • In a Cochrane review neither diet nor exercise alone decreased BMI in children ≤ 12 years of age. The combination of diet and exercise led to modest reductions in BMI. • Eating meals every day as a family* • Protective against purging and binge eating and frequent dieting • Increased consumption of fruits, vegetables, and grains • Beneficial for maintaining healthy weight and attitudes toward food Dietary Changes for Diabetes • Healthy diet (Mediterranean/DASH style - rich in monounsaturated fats) – decreases mortality • Increase amount and variety of seafood • Whole grains, fruits, veggies, low fat dairy (2 servings whole grains = 21% ↓ diabetes) • Increase potassium, calcium, Vitamin D, magnesium in food sources & B12 • Limit alcohol

questions.stem: A 37-year-old male with a BMI of 35 kg/m2 would like to lose weight without changing his diet but with daily moderate-intensity exercise. You tell him: 60 minutes Exercise • 5-7 times/wk, 30 minutes (45-60 min for weight loss) • 150 min/wkof moderateaerobic activity; 55-69% predicted maximum heart rate • Resistance training (5 major muscle groups) twice weekly • At least 1 hour daily for children* (reduce screen time to < 2 hour/day) • Advantages of exercise • Moderately beneficial for weight loss (even when added to diet)* • 9% decrease in cardiovascular risk for every 10,000 steps/day at baseline • Enhances insulin action in skeletal muscles • Decreases blood pressure • Maintenance of weight loss • Decreases risk of gestational diabetes and progression to diabetes • In type 1 diabetes: Athletesshould not participate in strenuous activity if glucose > 300 mg/dL or > 250 mg/dL with urine ketones* Weight Loss Maintenance • Monitor body weight at least once a week • Eat breakfast daily* • Maintain high levels of physical activity (200-300 min/wk) • Consume a reduced calorie diet • Face-to-face or telephone-delivered maintenance programs • Provide at least monthly contact with interventionist • Learn to eat when hungry and eat slowly • Learn to eat only to satiety (National Weight Control Registry: lost substantial weight and maintained for ~ 5 yr) Pharmacotherapy General Principles for Medications • Indicated for BMI ≥ 30 or ≥ 27 with comorbid conditions • Use only approved weight loss medications • Medications primarily effective if combined with lifestyle change • Monitor monthly for 3 months then every 3 months • Continue use only if weight loss is > 5% in 3 months • No evidence for hoodia, green tea, guar gum, raspberry extract, garcinia, guarana Pharmacologic Treatment in Prediabetes • Metforminand pioglitazone decrease insulin resistance but no data on CVD reduction in the absence of diabetes • Statins reduce CVD risk but increase diabetes risk • Delay onset of diabetes: • Lifestyle—most effective • Metformin (especially after gestational diabetes), acarbose, liraglutide, orlistat, pioglitazone, SGLT-2 inhibitors, Tianqi (Chinese herbal) • Pick medications that may promote weight loss • Topiramate for seizures or migraine prophylaxis • Bupropion (Wellbutrin) for depression • Topiramate or lisdexamfetamine(Vyvanse) for psychological treatments • GLP-1 analogs, SGLT-2 inhibitors, or metformin for diabetes

All of the following are common symptoms of hyperparathyroidism EXCEPT: Hyperparathyroid Treatment Presentation • Locate & remove tumor • Bone pain surgically • Depression • Frequent urination • Kidney Stones • Nausea • Loss of appetite Hyperparathyroidism Primary Secondary • Single benign adenoma (80%) • Typically due to vitamin D deficiency, chronic kidney disease, - Multiple benign tumors, rarely or other causes of low blood calcium parathyroid cancer • Manage underlying cause • Most people with primary disease are asymptomatic at the time of diagnosis • Remove the adenoma or overactive parathyroid glands Causes of Hypercalcemia Parathyroid hormone-related Granulomatous disease • Primary hyperparathyroidism, • Sarcoidosis, berylliosis, Tb Familial (MEN I or II) Hodgkin's lymphoma • Tertiary hyperparathyroidism Malignancy • Associated with chronic renal • PTHrP failure or vitamin D deficiency • Solid tumors, especially lung, Vitamin D-related head, and neck SCC and RCC • Vitamin D intoxication • Multiple myeloma (osteolysis) Causes of Hypercalcemia Medications Genetic disorders • Thiazide diuretics (mild)* • Familial hypocalciuric • Lithium hypercalcemia: mutated calcium-sensing receptor • Milk-alkali syndrome (antacids) • Vitamin A intoxication (analogs Other used to treat acne) • Immobilization, high bone turnover (Paget's disease) Other endocrine disorders • Recovery phase of • Hyperthyroidism rhabdomyolysis • Adrenal insufficiency • Acromegaly • Pheochromocytoma Hypoparathyroidism • Rare, only 900 cases per year in U.S. • Often post-surgical. • Treatment is to restore calcium & mineral balance with Calcium & Vitamin D supplementation.

questions.stem: A 33 yo presents with irregular menses and galactorrhea for 3 months. The remainder of her PMH and PE are unremarkable. Lab studies are within normal limits, except for a prolactin level of 310 mg/mL.The most appropriate next step in her work-up is: Prolactinoma – Diagnosis • Prolactin correlates with tumor size • Rule of 200’s - Prolactin level >200 is almost always a prolactinoma - < 25 is normal • Pregnancy test • Thyroid function studies (TSH and Free T4) • MRI with contrast or CT scan with coronal cuts • Formal visual field examination if >10 mm in size • Evaluation of remainder of pituitary function, if indicated Pituitary Abnormalities Sx depend on hormone secreted • Prolactin –Amenorrhea, galactorrhea, impotence • Growth hormone – Gigantism and acromegaly • Corticotropin – Cushing’s disease • TSH – Hyperthyroidism Evaluation of Sellar Masses MRI with and without gadolinium • Gadolinium contrast - Normal pituitary takes up gadolinium more than does CNS tissue - Microadenomas often take up gadolinium less than normal pituitary CT • Calcification in a craniopharyngioma or a meningioma is seen better by a CT than by MRI PET Scan • Uptake by adenoma was 2-3X greater than by craniopharyngiomas or meningiomas Differential Diagnosis for a Sellar Mass Benign Tumors Malignant Tumors • Pituitary adenoma (most common) • Primary • Craniopharyngioma - Germ cell tumor - Sarcoma • Meningiomas - Chordoma • Pituitary carcinoma - Metastatic - Lung - Breast Cushing’s Syndrome (Think  Cortisol) Primary features Other features • Myopathy, hirsutism, • Central obesity opportunistic infections, loss of • Ecchymoses libido • Plethora • Proximal weakness Treatment • Osteopenia/osteoporosis • Reducing steroid use, • Hypertension • Surgery, radiation, and • WBC >11.0 medications • Purple striae >1cm wide Cushing’s Syndrome • 10-15 per million in general population • Higher prevalence in patients with - Diabetes - Obesity - Hypertension - Osteoporosis • Peak Incidence in 25-40 yo Laboratory Diagnosis of Cushing’s Syndrome 1st confirm hypercortisolemia; 2nd determine the source • Cortisol levels (circadian cycling) - AM cortisol may be normal - Raised midnight cortisol • 24-hr urinary free cortisol - Not affected by obesity, drugs, medical conditions - Need to measure creatinine (ratio unreliable) - 4x normal unequivocal, lower uncertain • Midnight salivary cortisol - Raised in medical/psychiatric illness • Dexamethasone Suppression test Dexamethasone Suppression Test Dexamethasone acts like cortisol, lowers the amount of ACTH released by the pituitary gland Normal Cushing’s Syndrome Give Dexamethasone Adrenals Adrenals Pituitary Adrenals make makes less Pituitary still continue ACTH less makes less making cortisol ACTH making cortisol cortisol Addison’s Disease (Think cortisol &  aldosterone) Primary Clinical presentation • Atrophy or destruction of • Hyperpigmentation adrenal glands • Low blood pressure Secondary • Weight loss • N&V • Inadequate secretion of ACTH from pituitary gland • Muscle cramps • Irregular menses • Salt craving • Malaise, fatigue Addison’s Disease (Think cortisol &  aldosterone) Diagnostic Testing Treatment • Serum electrolytes • Replace cortisol • Blood glucose • Replace aldosterone • CBC • ACTH stimulation test • CT scan adrenals • MRI adrenals Summary Pearls Thyroid • TSH to start diagnosis • Be vigilant for thyroid disease in pregnancy Parathyroid • Focus on hyperparathyroidism (high Ca, high PTH) • Usually primary, remove the tumor Pituitary Masses • Sxdependent on specific hormone Addison’s Disease • Think low cortisol and aldosterone (Must Add some); questions.stem: Ms. Johnson, a 54-year-old woman, presents to the gastroenterology clinic with a history of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) that has been unresponsive to maximum proton pump inhibitor (PPI) therapy. She reports persistent heartburn, regurgitation, and nocturnal symptoms despite adhering to an optimized PPI regimen for the past six months. Ms. Johnson is otherwise healthy, with no significant comorbidities. GERD Diagnosis • No gold standard; EGD is to assess complications (SOR: A). ▪ Erosive esophagitis ▪ Stricture ▪ Barrett’s esophagus ▪ Cancer • EGD lacks adequate sensitivity in determining pathologic reflux (SOR B) Diagnostic Testing Esophageal manometry • is NOT recommended in the diagnosis of GERD • IS recommended in the pre-operative evaluation, e.g. Nissen fundoplication (SOR: C) 24-hour pH monitoring • is NOT recommended in the routine diagnosis of GERD and is NOT required in the presence of Barrett’s esophagus (SOR: B) • IS recommended in patients refractory to PPI therapy OR when the diagnosis is in question (SOR: C) Barium radiology: Limited usefulness; not recommended (SOR: A) Am J Gastroenterol. 2013; 108:308–328 Diagnostic Testing • Screening for Helicobacter pylori infection in GERD?

questions.stem: Your next step is: Pearls • Drug-induced parkinsonism can occur with medications overlooked as considered culprits (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, sodium valproate). • Atypical antipsychotics may cause parkinsonism: risperidone, olanzapine, et al. • Quetiapine, pimavanserin, clozapine have little extrapyramidal toxicity Pearls • Typical Symptoms of Parkinson Disease • Asymmetric Resting Tremor • Bradykinesia • Rigidity • Postural Instability (late) • Hypophonia • Loss of smell • Micrographia PD Treatment • Response to levodopa can be diagnostic • Carbidopa/Levodopa 25mg/100mg • Start low and increase every 1-2 weeks • Can take up to 2 months (sometimes immediate) Carbidopa/Levodopa (Sinemet) • Most effective med for gait (bradykinesia, rigidity); tremor response variable. • Carbidopa prevents peripheral breakdown of levodopa; >75 mg daily for max effect. • Begin 25/100 bid or tid; may use 25/250 as dose increased; avg patient needs about 500 to 1,000 mg L-dopa/day. Treating PD in the Elderly • Carbidopa/levodopa is the most effective medication for PD; optimize dose before adding other drugs • Anticholinergics and amantadine have little role in treating elderly PD patients. • COMT inhibitors and MAO inhibitors are very expensive for modest gain. • Dopamine agonist more likely to cause delusions/ hallucinations

questions.source: Session 21 — AAFP Board Prep Poll Question
questions.stem: A 28-year-old pregnant female, G3P2, is diagnosed with a DVT in her right lower extremity. The treatment of choice for this patient would be: VTE in Pregnancy  VTE risk increases 5-fold in pregnancy  Risk 1/1,600 pregnancies  PE is a leading cause of preg-associated mortality/ morbidity  20-50% have an underlying thrombophilia  History of VTE: 3-4 x increased risk for recurrence during preg VTE in Pregnancy • Risk of VTE overall, antepartum = postpartum and occurs with equal frequency throughout all trimesters • PE is more common in the postpartum period, especially 1st wk • D-dimer less useful in pregnancy (almost always positive) • Lower dose chest CTPA safe in pregnancy – If not available, do CXR – If CXR is normal, do V/Q scan – If CXR is abnormal, do full dose CT scan Anticoagulation in Pregnancy • Heparin and LMWH do not cross placenta and are safe in pregnancy SORT B • Unfractionated Heparin (UH) – Dosage requirements increase due to increases in heparin binding proteins, plasma volume, renal clearance and degradation by the placenta • Low-Molecular-Weight Heparin (LMWH) – Less bleeding, osteoporosis and HIT in non-pregnant pts compared to UH – Longer half-life – longer dosage intervals, but increased risk if epidural/spinal – Epidural anesthesia 12 hours after last dose of LMWH SORT C ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 196. ObstetGynecol2018;132(1):p e1–e17. Anticoagulation in Pregnancy • Warfarin- not for use in pregnancy – Crosses the placenta – Associated with harmful fetal effects – Use postpartum or in some patients with mechanical heart valves • DOACS not adequately studied in pregnancy • All anticoagulants are safe for breastfeeding mothers • LMWH and coumadin with good data • DOACs- limited data ACOG Bulletin #196-ObstetGynecol2018;132(1):p e1–e17. SORT B Anticoagulation in Pregnancy • Convert from LMWH to UH for last month of pregnancy • Compression stockings until anticoagulation resumed postpartum • Restart anticoagulation after delivery – 4-6 hours post vaginal delivery – 6-12 hours post C-section • May be bridged to warfarin or DOACs postpartum • Avoid paradoxical thrombosis and skin necrosis from early anti- protein C effect of warfarin by bridging with LMWH or UFH for 5 days and INR 2-3 x 2 days ACOG Bulletin #196-ObstetGynecol2018;132(1):p e1–e17. SORT B. VTE and COVID in pregnancy • If hospitalized, VTE prophylaxis while hospitalized continue for 2 weeks after discharge • No data on those managed as outpatients • National Institutes of Health COVID-19 Treatment Guidelines • ACOG: COVID-19 FAQs for Obstetrician-Gynecologists, Obstetrics Contraception if history of VTE • Avoid Estrogen • Use of progesterone is controversial • NIH: ok with first DVT, but not if recurrent • Depo Provera may have the highest risk but not well studied

questions.stem: A 48-year-old female with rheumatoid arthritis presents to your office complaining of two months of fatigue, and more recently dyspnea on exertion. Her Hgb=9.2 with normocytic, normochromic indices. You order iron studies, which show low iron, low TIBC and high ferritin. The best treatment for this patient would be: 12 Kinetic Approach to Anemia • Two reasons for anemia: – Decreased RBC production (reticulocyte < 2) • Lack of substrate • Marrow disorders/suppression • Decreased trophic hormones • Chronic illness/inflammation – Increased RBC loss • Hemolysis – inherited or acquired (reticulocyte > 2) • Blood loss – occult or obvious Morphologic Approach to Anemia • RBC indices – reflect cell size – Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) – Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH) – Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration (MCHC) – Red cell Distribution Width (RDW) • Use the RBC indices to direct your work-up of anemia Morphologic Approach to Anemia • Macrocytic (MCV >100) – Reticulocytosis (high RDW) – B and folate deficiencies 12 • PPIs and metformin can cause B12 deficiency – Myelodysplasia Courtesy: Wikimedia Commons – Alcohol abuse, liver disease, hypothyroidism Morphologic Approach to Anemia • Microcytic Anemia (MCV <80) – Iron deficiency – Decreased heme synthesis • lead, sideroblastic anemia – Decreased globin synthesis By Dr Graham Beards (Own work) [CC BY-SA 3.0 (thalassemia, hemoglobinopathy) (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0)], via Wikimedia Commons – Chronic illness/inflammation (uncommon) Morphologic Approach to Anemia • Normocytic, normochromic anemia – Acute blood loss – Acute hemolysis (retic >2) – Hypersplenism – Anemia of chronic disease (most common) Iron Deficiency Anemia • Low iron, high iron binding capacity, low ferritin • Identify WHY the patient is iron deficient! – Menstrual blood loss – GI malignancy/blood loss • Response to iron therapy: • Retic count 5-7 days • Hct 4-6 weeks • Iron stores 4-6 months *Low dose iron (15mg elemental iron)- effective, less side effects- SOR C Am Fam Physician.2018;98(7):437-442

questions.stem: A 3-year-old patient is brought to see you after moving to your community. He has Hgb SS disease. The parents ask you whether he needs to continue taking the prophylactic penicillin prescribed by another physician. You should recommend: Sickle Cell Disease • Most common single gene disorder in African-Americans • Diagnosed by hemoglobin electrophoresis • Due to homozygous abnormal hemoglobin S (SS disease) – Sickle SC disease (less severe disease, more retinal disease) – Sickle-Thal (variable) • Hemoglobin S is poorly soluble when deoxygenated and forms polymers that deforms the cells Sickle Cell Disease • Clinical Manifestations – Mod-severe anemia (hgb <8) w/ high retic count, normal/ high MCV – Vaso-occlusive crises – muscular, CVA, renal, priapism, retinopathy • Tx: IV narcotics if severe pain (SOR-B), NSAIDs if mild-moderate pain – Infections – pneumococcus, hemophilus, salmonella – Aplastic crisis – parvovirus B19 marrow suppression – Acute chest syndrome – pneumonia + infarct – Splenic sequestration crisis – acute anemia Sickle Cell Disease • Infection Prophylaxis – Immunizations • Strep pneumonia (PCV13 and 20) • Neisseria meningitides (Menactra and Men-B) • H. influenza, Type B • Hepatitis B • Influenza – Penicillin prophylaxis (for all types of SSD) • Pen V p.o. 125 mg BID from age 3 month to 3 years • Pen V p.o. 250 mg BID from age 3 to 5 years • After age 5 is controversial Sickle Cell Disease Annual Screening with Transcranial Doppler Ultrasound to evaluate risk of stroke age 2-16 years (SORT A) Bone Marrow/ Stem Cell Transplant-potentially curative Hydroxyurea: age 9-42 months or adults who have 3 or more crises in 12 months decrease vaso-occlusive effects and risk of acute chest syndrome (SORT B) Disease modifying therapy (less VOC, ACS, mortality, transfusion, hosp) Increases levels of HbF Initiation requires contraceptive counseling, CBC and reticulocyte q4w Risks: neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, gets into breast milk, teratogen Am Fam Phys 2015 Dec 15; 92(12): 1069-1076 Multiple Myeloma • Incidence: 1.6% of all cancer, 10% of hematologic cancer in U.S. • Risk factors: age >65, African Americans • Signs/ sx: anemia (73%) bone pain (58%) elevated creatinine (48%) hypercalcemia (28%) N/V, weakness, recurrent infections, weight loss Multiple Myeloma Dx: abnormal plasma cells in marrow (immature, atypical) monoclonal protein in serum/ urine bone lesions Work Up: • Labs: CBC w/ diff, Cr, LDH, beta-2 macroglobulin, immunoglobulin studies • Skeletal survey • BM biopsy AM Fam Physician. 2017 Mar 15:95(6): 373-383A. Multiple Myeloma Treatment: • Primary care: Bisphosphonate txwhen first diagnosed DVT prevention-DOAC or LMWH Infection prevention • Prophylactic ABX (TMP/ SMZ + quinolones 1st3 moof tx) • PCN for recurrent pneumococcal infections • Vaccines: PCV, Hib, flu IVIG for serious infections Avoid nephrotoxic meds Nutrition support Pain control • Referral to oncology: chemo, stem cell transplant AM Fam Physician. 2017 Mar 15:95(6): 373-383A. Hemophilia • Inherited bleeding disorders – Hemophilia A – Factor VIII deficiency – Hemophilia B – Factor IX deficiency (Christmas Disease) • X-linked recessive – predominantly males affected • Become symptomatic within first two years of life – Only 50% have bleeding with circumcision – 3-5% have bleeding in perinatal period Hemophilia • Bleeding sites – Muscles – Hematuria – Gastrointestinal – Epistaxis and oral cavity – Joints • Late joint destruction • Joints preserved by early initiation of factor concentrate treatments Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura “ Pentad” • Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia • Thrombocytopenia usually with purpura • Acute renal insufficiency (HUS) • Neurological symptoms (TTP) • Fever • Actually rare for all five to be present Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura • Causes – Usually idiopathic – ADAMTS13 deficiency • Protease of VWF – inhibitory auto-antibody • Long polymers of VWF attract platelets – Shiga-toxin from E. coli 0157:H7 – Collagen vascular diseases, cancer and transplants – Medications • Ticlopidine, clopidogrel, quinine, mitomycin, tacrolimus • Treatment – plasma exchange therapy curative Leukemia • Acute Leukemia – Acute Lymphoblastic – Children • Fever, bleeding, musculoskeletal symptoms, • Hepatosplenomegaly (75%), lymphadenopathy (60%) – Acute Myelogenous –Adults • Fever, fatigue, weight-loss, bleeding problems • Anemia, thrombocytopenia • Rouleaux formation on smear Leukemia • Chronic Leukemias – Adults • Most asymptomatic at time of diagnosis • Discovered with leukocytosis on blood count • Work Up: repeat CBC, smear, flow cytometry, onc referral • Chronic lymphocytic leukemia – 50% • Hepatosplenomegaly and lymphadenopathy • Chronic myelogenous leukemia – 20% • Splenomegaly Am Fam Physician.2014;89(9):731-738 Board Frequent Fliers • D Dimer only helpful for low pretest probability • if not CTPA for PE and doppler for DVT • DOAC preferred over coumadin (LMWH if cancer) • Dose adjust DOACs with renal disease • No coumadin in pregnancy (DOACs with insufficient data) • Anemia of Chronic DZ: treat underlying DZ • Macrocytic anemia- from PPIs due to B12 deficiency • Sickle Cell: transcranial U/S screening • Multiple Myeloma: start bisphosphonate tx • TTP pentad: fever, anemia, thrombocytopenia, AKI, neuro sx • Acute Leukemia: fever, bleeding • Chronic Leukemia: asymptomatic, elevated WBC-work up: repeat WBC, smear, flow cytometry, oncreferral Answers 1. A 2. D 3. A 4. C 5. D 6. C

questions.stem: A 65-year-old male patient of yours presents to the ED having had a seizure in his bedroom witnessed by his wife. She heard a cry and the fall, saw him stiffen and shake all over, and then become incontinent of urine. He was not arousable until he had been in the ED for several minutes. His seizure would be classified as: Definition A seizure is a transient occurrence of signs or symptoms due to abnormal excessive or synchronous neuronal activity in the brain, and can be either focal or generalized 10% of adults have a SZ during their lifetime • 1/3 will have a recurrence within 1 yr of an initial unprovoked SZ Classification of Seizures • Focal seizures (formerly partial) – Local – Ipsilateral propagation – Contralateral propagation – Secondarily generalized – Consciousness may or may not be impaired (formerly complex vs simple) Classification of Seizures • Generalized seizures – Non-convulsive (absence) • Typical (3/sec spike and slow waves on EEG) • Atypical (<3/sec spike and slow waves on EEG) – Convulsive • Myoclonic (very short muscle contractions) • Clonic (repeated jerking) • Tonic (stiffening) • Tonic-clonic (stiffening followed by jerking) – Grand Mal term is now outdated and no longer used • Atonic (“drop attacks” – loss of muscle tone) • PNES- Psychogenic Non-Epileptic Seizures (former pseudo-seizures)

questions.source: Session 26 — AAFP Board Prep Poll Question
questions.stem: The most likely cause of a new seizure in a patient >65 years old would be: Etiology of Seizures • Idiopathic – 62% overall ages • Stroke – 15% overall, 49% >age 60 • Brain tumor – 6% overall, 11% >age 60 • Head trauma • Intracranial infection • COVID • 55% more SZs in first 6 mo vs those who had influenza- stronger association in kids • Cerebral degeneration • Congenital brain malformations Family Medicine Today November 2022 • Inborn errors of metabolism “Provoked” Seizures • Seizures that occur within a medical setting, that, if removed, presumably the seizures would not occur • Metabolic derangements – Hypo and hyperglycemia – Hyponatremia – Hypocalcemia (usually neonates) – Renal failure and uremia “Provoked” Seizures • More rare metabolic causes – Hyperthyroidism – Acute intermittent porphyria • Cerebral anoxia – Arrest, anesthesia, drowning, CO – Syncope with brief hypoventilation (adolescents) • Drug toxicities/withdrawal – Alcohol – Benzodiazepines Epilepsy Epilepsy is a chronic condition characterized by at least two unprovoked seizures at least 24 hours apart.

questions.stem: A patient presents to you having had a single new unprovoked seizure with negative workup. Your first step in treatment would be: Starting Antiepileptic Drugs (AEDs) • Not needed if single new onset seizure or infrequent seizures • Immediate AED therapy compared to delay of tx pending a 2nd seizure is likely to reduce recurrence risk within the first 2 years (SORT B) but not improve quality of life (SORT C) • Immediate AED unlikely to improve prognosis over a longer term (> 3 years) (SORT B) • Treat only if there is a high risk of recurrence Neurology. 2015; 84(16): 1705 JAMA Neurol. 2018 75(3): 279-286 High Risk of Seizure Recurrence • Status epilepticus • Hx of brain injury • Brain lesion on neuroimaging • Focal neurological abnormalities • Intellectual disability • Abnormal EEG with epileptiform discharges • High-risk seizure types • Focal, absence, myoclonic, atonic; nocturnal SZ Am Fam Physician.2022;105(5):507-513 Antiepileptic Drugs • Start with AED monotherapy • Selection is individualized based on: – Relative efficacy – Other medications (enzyme induction) – Comorbid conditions – Potential adverse effects, tolerability, serious toxicity – Patient preferences and cost • No combination therapy until patient has failed two monotherapy trials • 1/3 of patients have uncontrolled epilepsy despite multiple agents • Monitor patients closely for effectiveness, side effects, and adherence Epilepsia. 2006 July; 47 (7): 1094-120 SORT A NeurolClin. 2010 Nov; 28 (4): 843-52 Classification of AEDs Broad Spectrum All seizure types Narrow Spectrum Focal or secondarily generalized seizures First Generation Less expensive More side effects and drug interactions Require monitoring of drug levels Second Generation More expensive Fewer side effects and interactions Monitoring often unavailable Antiepileptic Drugs First Generation Second Generation More complicated pharmacokinetics Fewer serious adverse effects Narrower therapeutic ranges Wider therapeutic Ranges Less expensive Lower Risk teratogenesis More expensive Examples: Exampes: Carbamazepine Levetiracitam Ethosuxamide Lamotrigine Phenobarbital Oxcarbazepine Phenytoin Gabapentin Primidone Pregabalin ValproicAcid Topiramate Antiepileptic Drugs: First-Line Monotherapy Seizure type < 16 years old 16-59 years old > 60 years Comments old Focal Oxcarbazepine Carbamazepine Gabapentin SORT A Levetiracetam Lamotrigine Phenytoin Zonisamide Generalized: Ethosuxamide No SORT A No SORT A Choose a Broad Absence ValproicAcid spectrum 2nd generation Generalized: No SORTA No SORTA No SORTA Recommendations Others are SORT C/D Am Fam Phys2017 July 15; 96 (2): 87-96 Toxicities of AEDs • Common toxicities with AEDs- 31% report adverse effects – Suicidality –2x risk compared to placebos – Neuro-toxicities – ataxia, dizziness, somnolence, fatigue, HA – Rash – wide spectrum from simple maculopapular rashes to Stevens-Johnson Syndrome – Liver enzyme – induction or inhibition Am Fam Physician.2022;105(5):507-513

questions.source: Session 26 — AAFP Board Prep Poll Question
questions.stem: A 46-year-old male smoker comes to you for a complaint of unilateral, cyclical, nocturnal headaches that last 30 minutes each. He has taken OTC meds but they have not been helpful. The best prophylactic medication for his headaches would be: Primary Headaches • Tension-type • Trigeminal Autonomic Cephalgia – Primary, unilateral headache in trigeminal nerve distribution – Ipsilateral autonomic symptoms – Cluster headaches are the most common • Migraines • Others: cough, exercise, sexual activity, etc Chronic Tension Headaches Bilateral, band like Tx: OTC analgesics • if >2x/week, increased risk of chronic daily HA • TCAs for prophylaxis (SOR B) • Butalbital and opioids should be avoided- increases risk of chronic daily HA Chronic Daily Headaches: 15 or more HA per month x 3 months • Can be due to chronic tension headaches or chronic migraines • Treatment: stop all abortive treatments, begin prophylactic treatments: • Non-pharmacologic: biofeedback/ relaxation, cognitive behavioral therapy (SOR B) • Pharmacologic: SOR A: topiramate, valproate SOR B: amitriptyline, gabapentin, tizanidine no effect: SSRIs, propranolol, botox Am Fam Physician 2014:89(8): 642-648. Cluster Headaches • Prevalence <1%, male> female 2-3:1 • Orbital or temporal unilateral pain Last 15 minutes to 3 hours • Ptosis, miosis, rhinorrhea, lacrimation or conjunctival injection • Triggers: alcohol, NTG, nitrates in foods, strong odors • Acute tx– oxygen and SQ/ intranasal triptans- SOR A • 2nd line: somatostatin receptor agonists, nasal lidocaine • Transitional tx: suboccipital steroid injxn- SOR A • Preventive treatment – Verapamil – tx of choice (240-320 mg per day or more)- SOR A – 2nd line: steroids, lithium, topiramate, melatonin – refractory: galcanezumab, neurostimulation- vagal/ deep brain Am Fam Physician.2022;105(1):24-32 Migraine Headache • Affects 18-26% women, 6-9% men in US • Pathophysiology – unclear – Cellular and blood-brain permeability changes inflammation of meninges and vasodilation – Genetic factors play a major role • Recurrent attacks in four phases – Prodrome: euphoria, depression, irritability, yawning – Aura: Visual – flickering lights, spots or lines Sensory – pins and needles, numbness Dysphasic speech – Headache – Postdrome – sudden head movement causes head pain ICHD-3 Criteria: Migraine without Aura 1. unilateral location 2. pulsating/ throbbing quality 3. moderate or severe pain intensity 4. *aggravation by or causing avoidance of routine physical activity (e.g. walking or climbing stairs) 1. nausea and/or vomiting 2. *photophobia and phonophobia E. Not better accounted for by another ICHD-3 diagnosis International Classification of Headache Disorders ICHD-3 Criteria: Migraine WITH Aura 1. at least one aura symptom spreads gradually over ≥5 minutes 2. two or more aura symptoms occur in succession 3. each individual aura symptom lasts 5-60 minutes 4. at least one aura symptom is unilateral 5. at least one aura symptom is positive 6. the aura is accompanied, or followed within 60 minutes, by headache Diagnosis of Migraine Headache • Clinical diagnosis – primarily history • Negative neurological examination • Neuroimaging unnecessary, except with – Unexplained abnormal neuro findings – Headaches that do not fit the strict definition – Choose Wisely Campaign (Radiology) • No benefit for EEG – Choose Wisely Campaign (Neurology) “Red Flags” for Neuroimaging • Rapidly increasing headache frequency • Lack of coordination, abnl neuro exam, papilledema • Headaches that awaken from sleep • Worse headache of life • Patients with cancer, HIV, Lyme disease • New onset of migraines over age 50 • Rapid onset with strenuous exercise • CT or MRI w/o contrast- test of choice (contrast if immunocompromised/ cancer, temporal arteritis, dissection, aneurysm) Am Fam Physician2013 May 15;87(10): 682-687 Acute Treatment of Migraine: First Line • Acetaminophen, NSAIDS • Triptans – serotonin 1b/1d agonists – All triptans have been found to be effective and well-tolerated – Avoid triptans (due to increased BP risk) in: • Familial hemiplegic migraine • Basilar migraine • Ischemic stroke • CAD, Prinzmetal’s angina, uncontrolled HTN • Pregnancy (relative contraindication) • Acetaminophen/aspirin/caffeine • Sumatriptan/naproxen Am Fam Physician. 2018 Feb 15; 97 (4): 243-251 Acute Treatment of Migraine: Second Line • Anti-emetics: dopamine antagonists • Metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, promethazine • Risk extrapyramidal adverse effects • Intranasal Dihydroergotamine (DHE) • Side effects: leg cramps, HTN, tinging in extremities • Contraindicated in pregnancy • Opioids: tramadol, codeine, meperidine, butorphanol • Moderate effectiveness but high abuse potential • Routine use not recommended Am Fam Physician. 2018 Feb 15; 97 (4): 243-251 Refractory Acute Migraine Parenteral Dihydroergotamine (DHE45) • Avoid for 24 hours after triptan • Severe N/V, HTN Magnesium sulfate IV: alternate tx for migraine with aura Dexamethasone IV: consider to decrease recurrence- prednisone taper x10-14 d Valproate IV: contradictory evidence Diphenhydramine: not recommended Opioids: use sparingly and infrequently Am Fam Physician. 2018 Feb 15; 97 (4): 243-251 Preventive Treatment of Migraine • Indications- less than 1 in 5 who qualify are receiving prevention – Headaches significantly interfere with usual activities – Overuse of acute therapies – Adverse events with acute therapies – Patient preference – Certain migraine variants that mimic CVAs • Hemiplegic migraines • Basilar migraines • Prolonged aura Preventive Treatment of Migraine • First line agents (if no contraindications): SORT A • valproic acid • frovatriptan (menstrual migraines) • propranolol, timolol, metoprolol • topiramate • If not effective after 2-3 months, adjust dose • If max dose or adverse effects, change med *If no single first line agent effective/tolerable, consider combination • Second line agents: amitriptyline, atenolol, nadolol, naratriptan, questions.source: Session 41 — AAFP Board Prep Poll Question
questions.stem: Research has shown that the optimal treatment approach for children with ADHD is: Autism Spectrum Disorders In DSM-5, four separate diagnoses are combined into a single condition known as Autism Spectrum Disorders They previously were: 1. Autistic Disorder 2. Asperger’s Disorder 3. Childhood Disintegrative Disorder 4. Rett Syndrome Autism Spectrum Disorders Diagnostic Criteria A neurodevelopmental disorder defined by social communication impairments and restricted, repetitive behaviors Social Communication Restricted, Repetitive Behaviors Impairments Stereotyped or repetitive motor movements, use of Deficits in social-emotional reciprocity objects, or speech Insistence on sameness, inflexible adherence to routines, or ritualized patterns of behavior Deficits in nonverbal communicative behaviors used for social interaction Highly restricted, fixated interests that are abnormal in intensity or focus Deficits in developing, maintaining, and understanding Hyper-or hyporeactivity to sensory input or unusual relationships interest in sensory aspects of the environment Autism Spectrum Disorders (ASD) • Epidemiology • Among children aged 8 years in the US, the estimated prevalence of ASD has increased from approximately 1.1% in 2008 to 2.3% in 2018 • Potential explanations for increased prevalence • Changes in diagnostic criteria (broader) • Increased awareness • Greater access to services • Prevalence of ASD is higher in boys (3.7%) than in girls (0.9-1.5%) at 8 years of age • Possible reasons for increased prevalence in boys • Girls are better able to minimize ASD symptoms (“camouflaging”) • Diagnostic tools are less sensitive to the presence of ASD among females Autism Spectrum Disorders • Pathogenesis and Pathophysiology • A variety of genetic and environmental factors have been associated with ASD, but none are specific • Estimated heritability: 81% • Environmental factors: 14-22% • Medication use during pregnancy and risk of ASD • Valproic acid: 4.4% with exposures during pregnancy compared with the risk of 1.5% without exposure • Antidepressant: Studies are confounded by the indication for antidepressant use and the association between maternal depression and ASD • Vaccines: A large body of research refutes any linkage between vaccines and ASD Autism Spectrum Disorders ytidibromoC ADHD 28% in ASD vs 8.5% in the general population Intellectual disability 23% in ASD vs 0.7% in the general population Anxiety disorder(s) 20% in ASD vs 7% in the general population Depressive disorder(s) 11% in ASD vs 5% in the general population Epilepsy 21% in people with ASD and intellectual disability vs 0.8% in general population 8% in those with only ASD vs 0.8% in general population Constipation 22% in ASD Sleep problems 13% in ASD vs 5% in the general population Autism Spectrum Disorders • Screening • AAP recommends that all children be screened at 18 and 24 months of age for ASD • USPSTF concluded that evidence was insufficient to recommend screening for ASD in young children for whom no concerns were raised by their parents • Screening tools • The Modified-Checklist for Autism in Toddlers, Revised (M-CHAT-R) • Children whose M-CHAT-R total score is higher than 2 are considered at risk for ASD and require follow-up questions (M-CHAT-R/F) Autism Spectrum Disorders • Diagnostic Assessment • The gold standard is clinical consensus, defined as “agreement within a multidisciplinary professional team based on a detailed developmental history and observation of the individual’s behaviors using standardized diagnostic tools” • Autism Diagnostic Interview, Revised (ADI-R): a semi-structured interview with the parent(s) • Autism Diagnostic Observation Schedule-2 (ADOS-2): a semi-structured direct observation of a child’s behavior • The AAP recommends genetic testing for individuals who are diagnosed with ASD Autism Spectrum Disorders • Behavioral Interventions • Early intervention based on well-established behavioral analytic principles • Focus on behavior change based on understanding and changing the predisposing environmental conditions or events that reinforce specific behaviors • Associated with an improvement in children’s language, play, and social communication • At least 25 hours per week • In children who have no or mild cognitive impairment, social deficits and friendship quality typically worsen during adolescence • Social skills training delivered in a group format is associated with modest effects on social competence Autism Spectrum Disorders • Pharmacotherapy • Risperidone and aripiprazole are currently approved by the FDA for irritability and aggression in patients with ASD • Risperidone: ES 1.10 • Aripiprazole: ES 1.20 • Stimulants (methylphenidate) and nonstimulants (atomoxetine and guanfacine) have demonstrated effectiveness in ADHD symptoms in ASD. Methylphenidate: ES 0.6 Atomoxetine: ES 0.5 Guanfacine: ES 1.2 However, people with both ADHD and ASD are more likely to experience behavioral activation with psychostimulants compared with individuals without ASD Autism Spectrum Disorders • Prognosis • Adults with ASD less frequently live independently and are more frequently unemployed than people without ASD • Better cognitive abilities during childhood are associated with higher levels of independence, education, and employment later in life but are not associated with higher rates of friendship or well-being reported by caregivers • Premature mortality rates are approximately 2-fold higher for individuals with ASD than for the general population Obsessive Compulsive Disorder (OCD) Diagnostic Criteria • Presence of obsessions, compulsions, or both • The obsessions or compulsions are time-consuming (e.g., take more than 1 hour per day) or cause clinically significant distress or impairment in social, occupational, or other important areas of functioning Obsessive Compulsive Disorder (OCD) Diagnostic Criteria • Obsessions • Recurrent thoughts, impulses, or images that cause anxiety or distress • These thoughts, impulses, or images are experienced as intrusive and go beyond excessive worry about real-life problems • They are not related to another mental disorder • Compulsions • Ritualistic behaviors or mental acts that are performed in response to an obsession • These behaviors are excessive and performed to decrease anxiety or distress or avoid some dreaded event • They are not realistically connected to those dreaded events OCD: Common Symptoms Compulsions Obsessions • Checking 62% • Contamination 48% • Washing 46% • Doubt 47% • Need for symmetry 40% • Symmetry 45% • Counting 30% • Fear of aggression 36% • Somatic obsessions 35% (Hoarding is now a separate • Sexual obsessions 22% disorder in DSM-5) Obsessive Compulsive Disorder (OCD) • Epidemiology • Lifetime prevalence: 1-2% • Adults: men and women are equally affected • Children: boys>girls • Mean age of onset Males: 6-15 years of age Females: 20-29 years of age • Comorbidity • MDD: 67% • Tourette’s Disorder: 5-7% Obsessive Compulsive Disorder (OCD) • Treatment • Antidepressants (SSRIs or clomipramine) • High-dose • Delayed-onset of action • Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) Key Points for the Exam • Comorbidity is common in ADHD • ADHD must be present in more than 1 setting • Medication plus behavior modification: best ADHD treatment • ADHD adults are primarily inattentive • Treatment of OCD often requires high-doses of serotonergic medications Answers 1. D 2. A 3. B 4. B Supplementary Slides Oppositional Defiant Disorder (ODD) • Pattern of negativism, hostility, defiance • Angry/irritable mood: ‾ Often loses temper ‾ Touchy & easily annoyed ‾ Angry & resentful • Argumentative/defiant ‾ Often argues with adults ‾ Deliberately annoys ‾ Blames others ‾ Actively defies rules • Vindictiveness ‾ Spiteful and vindictive, holds grudges Treatment of ODD • There is no medication for ODD • There is medication for comorbid conditions that may exacerbate ODD • Parent training, behavioral interventions Conduct Disorder • A repetitive and persistent pattern of behavior in which the basic rights of others or major societal norms are violated for at least 6 months. Conduct Disorder • Aggression to people and animals • Destruction of property • Deceitfulness or theft • Serious violations of rules • The disturbance causes clinically significant impairment • Difficult to treat ‾ May require off-label mood stabilizers ‾ May require inpatient treatment, if severe Disruptive Mood Dysregulation Disorder (DMDD) • New Diagnosis in DSM-5 • Non-episodic temper outbursts > 3x a week • Persistently irritable or angry mood in all settings • Diagnosis only made between the ages of 6 and 18 • No symptoms of mania or hypomania Disruptive Mood Dysregulation Disorder (DMDD) • No treatment guidelines yet • Mood stabilizers sometimes helpful • Treat comorbid conditions (ADHD, etc.) • Behavioral and family interventions have been used Specific Learning Disorder • Significant discrepancy between school level, chronological age, intelligence, and academic performance or achievement • This discrepancy significantly interferes with academic and/or social functioning • Treatment: educational modifications • Medication: only if a comorbidity exists that requires medication use Common Learning Disorder Deficits • Developmental speech/language disorders • Articulation, receptive/expressive language • Academic skills disorders • Dyslexia (reading), dyscalculia (math), dysgraphia (writing) • Nonverbal learning disability • Visual-spatial, visual-motor, sensory, motor • Central auditory processing deficit • Distorted or incomplete auditory messages

questions.stem: You are caring for a 5-month-old child who developed intussusception. The mother is still exclusively breast feeding. How long must the child fast prior to surgery ASA Guidelines for Preoperative Fasting Ingested material Minimum hours of fasting Clear liquids 2 Breast milk 4 Formula, milk, light meal 6 Fatty foods / meat 8 ASA (American Society of Anesthesiologists) Preoperative Evaluation in Older Patients • Cognitive status • Patient’s life goals • PHQ-2 (PHQ-9 if positive) • Risk Analysis Index (score of 30 = frail; score of 40 = very frail) • Demographic factors (Age, Sex) • Comorbidities (Cancer, Weight loss, Renal failure, HF, Loss of appetite, Dyspnea at rest, Cognitive decline) • Facility residence • Level of independence (4 ADLs) • Social and living situation Risk Reduction • Pre- and post-operative exercise therapy / Physical therapy • Assess for delayed skin healing and surgical site infection risk: • Smoking, Diabetes, Obesity, Malnutrition, Chronic skin disease • Treat preexisting infections • Provide nutritional supplementation 7-14 days preoperatively • Assess for dental infections • Treat dental abscess prior to surgery • Smoking cessation (Need to quit smoking 8 weeks prior to surgery) • Counseling and cessation therapy should be offered to all patients using nicotine products (including electronic delivery systems) (SOR: C) • Some surgeons will not do elective surgery if the patient smokes Medical Management in the Perioperative Period and Postoperative Management Diabetes in surgery Increased risk of infection and postop CV morbidity and mortality • Stop GLP-1 receptor agonists a week before surgery • Hold oral medications • Insulin management • Poor preoperative control leads to poor outcomes, so control should be addressed prior to surgery • Continue usual diabetes regimen* and minimize fasting • Doses of long-acting insulins should be continued at 50-100% of usual dose • Continue insulin pump basal dose • Hold scheduled short-acting insulins • Utilize short-acting, sliding scale insulin to maintain blood glucose at 140- 180* mg/dL (high quality evidence, strong recommendation)** Statins • Statin benefits • Lipid-lowering • Reduce vascular inflammation • Improve endothelial function • Stabilize atherosclerotic plaques (reduce 30-day MI & death) • Statin therapy (lovastatin and fluvastatin longer acting) • Perioperative initiation is reasonable in patient undergoing vascular surgery • Consider in patients with other indications for statin therapy* (Patients having PCI and CABG have decreased mortality if they take statin on day of surgery—equivalent of 20 mg atorvastatin) • Try to start at least 4 weeks prior to procedure • Should be continued in patients already taking them because risk of CV events sharply increases if stopped -blockers • In patients on beta blockers: continue before, during, and after surgery • Reduce cardiac oxygen demand • If they have been used for at least 4 weeks prior to surgery (SOR: A) • Used for known ischemic heart disease undergoing vascular surgery • If beta blockers have not been used for at least 1 week, initiation may be harmful (SOR: B) • Decreased MI, but increased stroke, hypotension, bradycardia, and death • Still appropriate prior to and after CABG Aspirin • POISE-2 Trial – Stop aspirin 5-7 days prior to surgery when not used for thromboembolism prophylaxis. Safe to continue through surgery if needed • No reduction in death or nonfatal MI in non-cardiac surgery • Whether on aspirin already or started prior to surgery • Bleeding more common with aspirin (hazard ratio = 1.23) • Safety of aspirin withdrawal in patients with prior coronary artery stenting is still questionable, so may continue in these patients • Dual Antiplatelet Therapy (DAPT)—aspirin should be continued when* • < 4-6 weeks after bare metal stent • < 1 year after drug-eluting stent • If stopping clopidogrel or ticagrelor (5 days) or prasugrel (7 days) continue aspirin

questions.stem: A 49 yo presents with recurrent episodes of hematuria and flank pain. Spiral CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis reveals urolithiasis. She eventually passes the stone into a urine strainer. Analysis of the stone reveals calcium oxalate. Dietary recommendations would include: Kidney Stones – Risk Factors RISK FACTORS SYMPTOMS • Male Gender • Abdominal Pain • Age (to 65) Renal colic • Low urine volume Sudden; not relieved • Situational; Geography Radiating pain to back or groin • Heredity • Hematuria • Diet (high Na) • Medications Meds Associated w/ Stone Formation • Triamterene and the sulfonamides (poor solubility) • Calcium and vitamin D supplements (hypercalciuria) • Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (increases urinary pH, thereby increasing calcium phosphate precipitation) • Indinavir, a protease inhibitor, (urinary crystallization) • NOT HCTZ (Increases Calcium reabsorption) Imaging Options Renal Colic Suspected (based on hx/pe) Pregnant, Gallbladder dz or Gyn Hx of radio-opaque Everyone else *A strategy beginning with cause suspected renal calculi ultrasonography (including point-of-care ultrasonography performed by an * emergency physician) can help many patients avoid CT and its associated radiation. (Level of Evidence = 1b–) Could consider bedside US if not obese Ultrasound X-ray IV Non- Pyelogram If Xray normal, but contrast you still have (only if no CT helical CT concerns for stone. available) http://www.aafp.org/afp/2015/0115/p132a.html Management Options <4 mm ~98% pass on their own in 1-2 wks Analgesia 5-10 mm Alpha-blockers (tamsulosin or >10 mm nifedipine) ~53% pass on their own unlikely to have any benefit Indication: for intervention: Strain urine  evidence of persistent UROLOGY obstruction F/U KUB q 1-2 wks Consult  failure of stone progression (SOR C)  persisting colic Urology if not passed in 2-4 wks ESWL or ureteroscopy RTC signs of sepsis Practice Recommendations •Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are the first choice for pain relief in patients with kidney stones (A) •Alpha blockers are the first choice for medical expulsive therapy in patients with kidney stones. (A) •Patients should increase daily fluid intake to 2.5 to 3 L per day to prevent recurrence of kidney stones. (A) •Thiazides, potassium citrate, or allopurinol should be prescribed after recurrence of calcium stones, even in the absence of metabolic abnormalities.
